1|publisher=Ritto Town|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994}}}} * Dictionaries ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=Kotsuki Taisha|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 25 Place names in Shiga Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490251|ref={{Harvid|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=Hiroshi Matsumura|year=2002|chapter=Kotsuki Taisha Tumulus Group|title=continued Dictionary of Japanese Kofun |publisher={{jalink|Tokyodo Publishing|Q11524645}}|isbn=978-4490105995|ref={{Harvid|Kotsuki Taisha Kofun Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Kotsuki clan|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names Popular version|publisher={{jalink|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|Q4346755}}|isbn=9784642014588|ref={{Harvid|Kotsuki clan (ancient clan)|2010}}}}* Others ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kotsuki Taisha|title={{jalink|Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial|1912}}}} *** [{{NDLDC|1088278/85}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Middle"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 85-86. ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikiuchisha research group|author=Shigeki Uno|chapter=Kotsuki Taisha|year=1981|title=Shikiuchisha research report Volume 12|publisher={{jalink|Kogakukan University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Kotsuki Taisha (Shikiuchisha)|1981}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}}edited|author=Nippo Uno|year=1986|chapter=Kotsuki Taisha/Kotsuki Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 5 Yamashiro/Omi|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022151|ref={{Harvid|Kotsuki Taisha/Kotsuki Shrine (Gods)|1986}}}}** {{Wikicite|reference={{cite journal|Japanese book|author=Nobuyoshi Tanaka, Saburo Tamura, Yoshida Yanagiji |date=2002-04 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/41936 |title=About math doctor Imao Otsuki (research on the history of mathematics) |journal=Kokyuroku of the Institute for Mathematical Analysis |ISSN=1880-2818 |publisher=Kyoto University Institute for Mathematical Sciences |volume=1257 |pages=181-185 |hdl=2433/41936 |CRID=1050282677273315968 |ref={{Harvid|About Math Doctor Imao Otsuki|2002}}}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|Mr. Otsuki|Q11462133}} * {{jalink|Otsuki Shrine|Q11462132}} === External link === {{Commonscat}} * Old {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Other names = Ozuesha, Kozue Shrine, Kozue Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 5th|Q2550}} (Kozue Festival) |Shinto ritual = Flower umbrella dance (May 5th) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Kotsuki Taisha|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Otsuki-taisha torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]]'''Kotsuki Taisha''' The main shrine"/>) is located in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ritto City|Q857310}}. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. {{jalink|Divine Crest}} is "Kakudari wisteria" and "Maya no Usagi" [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Otsuki Taisha] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency). . The ``{{jalink|Sanyare Odori of Omi Konan}}'' held within the temple grounds is designated as one of {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}} which is {{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Rakubetsuou||Ochibetsu no Mikoto}} (Ochiwake no Mikoto) Explanation board inside the precincts. *: Also written as ``Rakubetsu no Mikoto'' or ``Sobetsu no Mikoto''. He was the {{jalink|prince|prince|Q12886381}} of the 11th {{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}} and the ancestor of {{jalink|Kotsukiyama-kun|Q11462129}} ({{jalink|Kotsuki clan|Q11462133}}). Depending on the literature, the notation ``{{jalink|Issokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324}}'' is also seen, but Issokubetsu no Mikoto is a half-brother of Ochibetsu no Mikoto. * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Oonamuchi no Mikoto}} *: In the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, there was a {{jalink|Tendai sect|Q668188}} Gakuon-ji Temple (Kakuon-ji Temple) adjacent to the precincts of Otsuki Taisha Shrine; It is presumed that it was {{jalink|solicited}} and {{jalink|enshrined|Q11412524}} from {{jalink|Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|Hiyoshi Nishimoto Shrine}}. ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, there is one deity enshrined. At Kotsuki Taisha Shrine, two wooden seated statues of male deities (Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue and Den Omi Taka no Mikoto statue) are said to have been made in the early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}}, and by this time the enshrined deities were the current two. === History === The detailed founding date is unknown, but according to the shrine's legend, it was built in {{jalink|Ancient|Q41493}} in {{jalink|Kurita County|Q6446084}} (currently {{jalink|Kusatsu City|Q386677}}). Our company's origins date back to the time when {{jalink|Otsukiyama-kun|Q11462129}} (Otsukiyama-kun), a powerful family from the Ritto City|Q857310}} area, enshrined his ancestral deity, {{jalink|Rakubetsu-o||Ochibetsu no Mikoto}}.[http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency Kotsuki Taisha].Otsukiyama-kun was an ancient powerful family in Kurita County, and it is said that he also presented {{jalink|Uname|Q11644506}} to the {{jalink|Imperial Court (Japan)|Q30253177|Imperial Court}}. In addition to the Kotsuki Taisha tumulus group remaining within the Otsuki Taisha campus, the surrounding area is the {{jalink|Shimotoyama Tumulus|Q60990033}} (a tangible {{jalink|cultural property designated by Ritto City)}. |Q858308}}), the Jiyama Tumulus, and the Oka Ruins (Kurita{{jalink|gunga|Q11643230}} ruins) remain, and these are considered to be historical sites related to Kotsukiyama-kun. Based on these, it is assumed that Otsukiyama-kun was a member of the Kurita County {{jalink|gunji|Q4152149}} class. Otsukiyama-kun was {{jalink|Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}15th year ({{jalink|873|Q30369}})"Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated December 2, 15th year of Jogan (873). In , he moved to {{jalink|Heiankyo|Q751907}} and later served the {{jalink|Imperial Court (Japan)|Q30253177|Imperial Court}} as the {{jalink|Kotsuki clan|Q11462133}} ({{jalink|official|Q11452088}} family).In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushu}}, the divine names such as ``Kojogami'', ``Kojogami'', and ``Shōjogami'' were used in the 5th year of Jogan ({{jalink|863|Q25052}}) {{jalink|Shinkai|Q11591025}} was ranked Jugoinoge (Junior Fifth Rank) in 7th year of Jogan ({{jalink|865|Q30350}}) and to the lower rank of Junior Fourth Rank (Junior Fourth Rank) in {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}}11 ({{jalink|911|Q24062}}){{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}.{{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) In the Kishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|Shinmeicho}}, "Kotsuki Taisha" is written in {{jalink|Omi Province|Q843882}}{{jalink|Kurita County|Q6446084}}, It is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} along with its associated shrine {{jalink|Otsuki Shrine|Q11462132}} (currently {{jalink|Kusatsu City|Q386677}} Aochi Town). {Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}The ``taisha'' in the company name ``Kotsuki Taisha'' appears to be to distinguish it from the Otsuki Shrine. {{Harv|About the mathematician Dr. Kotsuki Imao|2002}}. . The company name is pronounced as ``Wotsuki no Ohoyashiro'' {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Additionally, two wooden male god seated statues ({{jalink|Important Cultural Properties|Q1188622}}) made in the early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}} of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} have survived to this day.After the Otsuki clan moved to the center, the head of the shrine was revered and renovated by the {{jalink|Aochi clan|Q11661125}}, who had a base in the vicinity, and the current inner palace of the main shrine was built by Motoji Aochi in October 1281|Q5537}}.. {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} In April 2013 ({{jalink|1343|Q6072}}), a four-legged gate was built by Shigeyori Aochi (although it does not exist, only the ridgepole was used as the main hall) {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shinouchi Shrine)|1981}}. According to "{{jalink|Sono Tairyaku|Q11422787}}" , in December of the same year, he was granted the ultimate rank of Shoichii upon the application of Shigeyori Aochi (Shigeyori Minamoto) {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. After that, in {{jalink|Eisho|Q1193577}}16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}), the current main shrine (Important Cultural Property) was rebuilt by Makoto Aojima. However, the Aochi clan fell into decline during the {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}} with the decline of the master family {{jalink|Rokkaku clan|Q2975342}}.In {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}13 ({{jalink|1608|Q6758}}) 3}} There was a donation of rice fields from the lord {{jalink|Ujitetsu Toda|Q3530228}}, and thereafter it was relieved by the {{jalink|black seal|Q11678034}}{{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}9) {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534} }, it was ranked as {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} and was promoted to {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in {{jalink|1881|Q7808}} (Meiji 14). === God level === * Record of {{jalink|Divine Rank|Q11591025}} dedication in the {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}} era ** {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 5th year ({{jalink|863 years|Q25052}}) December 3rd, Jugoinoge ("{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated December 3, 863. - The notation is "Shojo God". ** December 5th, 7th year of the Jogan era ({{jalink|865|Q30350}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper middle fifth rank (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') - The spelling is "Kojogami". ** October 9, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}} 6th year ({{jalink|882|Q23824}}), from the upper middle fifth rank to the upper middle fifth rank {{Sub|Mama}} (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') - The spelling is ``Koshogami''. Is it a mistake of Shogoinoge? {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * After Six Kingdoms History** {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} February 2nd, 11th year ({{jalink|911|Q24062}}), Junior Fourth Rank Lower ({{jalink|Nihon Kiryaku|Q1053542}}) - The spelling is ``Koshogami''. ** {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} December 2nd year ({{jalink|1343|Q6072}}), Shoichii ("{{jalink|Sono Tairyaku|Q11422787}}") - The notation is "Kozuesha". === Precincts === * Main shrine ({{jalink|Important cultural property|Q1188622}}) - Rebuilt in the late {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. The roof is {{jalink|Japanese cypress bark |Q90373477}}. The overall structure is simple but strong, with thick {{jalink|wood splitting}} and few carved decorations, as well as old-style boat elbows for the kumihimo. The copy of the ridge plate and the ink calligraphy on the ridgepole mention that it was rebuilt by Makoto Aojima in {{jalink|Eisho|Q1193577}}16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}), and the style suggests that it dates back to that time. Along with the main shrine, two ridge plates have also been passed down, each marking the replacement of the roof in {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}8 ({{jalink|1723|Q6921}}) and {{jalink|Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}12 ({{jalink|1815|Q6948}}). A palace is placed in the inner sanctum of the main hall, and two wooden seated statues of male deities (Important Cultural Properties) are enshrined within the palace. The palace has an ink calligraphy dated to {{jalink|Koan|Q389180}}4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), which is older than the main shrine. This main shrine, along with the palace and two ridge plates (both designated), have been designated as important cultural properties.* The Hall - Built in {{jalink|1956|Q5221}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}31). The roof is made of cypress bark. * Worship hall - Reconstruction of {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}. {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri |Q855025}}, the roof is tiled {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. * Ichino Torii - Built in {{jalink|1930|Q18787}} (1930). Regarding the torii, there is an old record that it was built in {{jalink|Enkyo|Q1183728}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1745|Q7586}}) near the current {{jalink|Meishin Expressway|Q904481}}, and its foundation stone was relocated to Otabisho and is from the stone marker next to the existing Ichi-no-Torii. . * Otabisho (Wakamiyasha) - Located in Oka, Ritto City, adjacent to the Oka ruins ({{jalink|Kurita-gun#gunga|Q6446084|Kurita-gunga ruins}}) ({{Coord|35|00|37.81 |N|135|59|07.23|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Otabisho}}). * Kotsuki Taisha Tumulus Group - Within the precincts are {{jalink|circular tombs| Q11394747}}・{{jalink|Hangular tomb|Q11504353}}There are over 10 {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha kofun group (Zoku kofun)|2002}}.** Mound No. 1 - The largest round tomb among the tombs, with a diameter of 25 meters. The stone remaining on the mound indicates the possibility of {{jalink|horizontal stone chamber|Q11543435}}{{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha kofun group (Zoku kofun)|2002}}. ** Mound No. 10 - Located on the ridge west of our company grounds. It is a rectangular tomb measuring 15.5 meters from north to south and 13.5 meters from east to west, and is said to have been constructed in the latter half of the 5th century. {{jalink|1990|Q2064}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}2) and {{jalink|1999|Q2091}} (Heisei 11) Excavations were carried out twice in 2002), and two split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins, an iron sword and a comb, and a Sue ware from the surrounding moat were unearthed. The details of the other burial mounds are not clear {{Sfn|Otsuki Taisha Tumulus Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}. The people buried in these burial mounds are believed to be members of the Otsukiyama clan. === Setsusha === * Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine) - Enshrined on the right side facing the main shrine. * Hiyoshi Shrine (Sanno Shrine) - Enshrined on the left facing the main shrine. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined next to the approach. * Ryuosha - Enshrined on top of the Kotsuki Taisha burial mound. * Outside company ** Junisho Shrine - Enshrined in Mt. Shimoto, Ritto City ({{Coord|35|00|17.43|N|135|59|28.81|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Outside Shrine: Jūnisho Shrine}}). The precincts are said to be the site of Gakuonji Temple (Kakuonji Temple). *** Shrines within the precincts of Junisho Shrine: Bentensha, Gionsha ** Hachiman Shrine File:Otsuki-taisha haiden.JPG|Shrine of worship File:Otsuki-taisha Ryuou-sha.JPG|Legendary ancient tomb stone (front) and Ryuou-sha shrine in the precincts File:Otsuki-taisha ichinotorii.JPG|Ichino Torii File:Otsuki-taisha otabisho.JPG|Otabisho (Wakamiyasha) File:Otsuki-taisha Yasaka-sha.JPG|Yasakasha (Gionsha) File:Otsuki-taisha Hiyoshi-sha.JPG|Hiyoshisha (Sannosha) File:Otsuki-taisha Inari-sha.JPG|Inari-sha File:Junisho Shrine (Kotsuki Taisha shrine).JPG|Junisho Shrine (outside shrine) === Main festivals === The annual festival held on {{jalink|May 5th|Q2550}} is commonly known as the ``''Kozue Festival''''. In ancient times, the first day of the rabbit was {{jalink|Uzuki|Q9092155}}, but since {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}42), the funeral ceremony has been held on May 5th.[http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Otsuki Taisha] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency). . The {{jalink|Hanagasa Odori|Q11615628|Hanagasa Odori}}, which is the main event of the festival, is currently held in rotation in five regions, but {{jalink|Eitoku|Q1145 According to a board dated 805}}2 ({{jalink|1382|Q6335}}), the history of Ritto is said to have been carried out by an influential family called the ``Sakakimoto family'' in ancient times. Volume 4|1994|p=281}}.During the festival, a mikoshi (portable shrine) is carried in procession to {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}} near the Oka Ruins (Kurita Gunga Ruins), and Hanagasa Odori (Flower Umbrella Dance) is dedicated at several locations, including in front of the main shrine of Otsuki Taisha Shrine and Otabisho. The centerpiece of this Hanagasa Odori is a child actor wearing a charming costume, followed by an adult holding a kasahoko (flower umbrella), who dance and sing in a spectacular manner. This is said to be a modernized version of the Taiko drum dance. The Kozue Festival was selected as an intangible folk cultural property (selected intangible folk cultural property) in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}} in {{jalink|1988|Q2426}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}63) and was designated as an intangible folk cultural property (selected intangible folk cultural property) for which measures such as recording should be taken in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. Volume 4|1994|p=281}}, {{jalink|2020|Q25337}} ({{jalink|Reiwa|Q62609017}}2) includes festivals in Kusatsu City and ``{{jalink|Sanyare in Omi Konan. The dance}} has been designated as a national important intangible folk cultural property. Cultural Affairs Council Report (Designation of Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties, etc.)] (Agency for Cultural Affairs press release, January 17, 2020) . Furthermore, "Omi Konan Sanyare Odori" will be held on November 30th, {{jalink|2022|Q49625}} (Reiwa 4).{{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7 809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}} It was designated as one of the {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}}. === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Main shrine (1 attached palace, 2 ridge tags) (building) *: Built in the late {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. {{jalink|1958|Q5253}} Designated on May 14, 1958 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|102|1438|Kotsuki Taisha main shrine}}. * 2 wooden seated male god statues (sculpture) *: {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}}. Two statues, the Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue and the Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue. {{jalink|1997|Q2088}} Designated on June 30, 1997 {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|5159|Wooden Seated Statue of the Male God (Denrakubetsu no Mikoto)}}{{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|5160|Wooden Seated Statue of the Male God (Dennen Omi Takamikoto)}}. ** The main deity statue, Denrakubetsu no Mikoto, is made of {{jalink|Hinoki|Q917187}} wood and is 60.6 cm in height. His belongings, the scepter and scepter, were made of a different material, but they were lost. He wears a crown on his head and a vermillion robe.{{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=281}}. ** The statue of Omi Takamiko is made of a single piece of cypress and is 48.8 centimeters tall. His belongings, the scepter and scepter, were made of a different material, but they were lost. He wears a wide crown, a beard, and a green robe.{{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=281}}. === Important intangible folk cultural property === * Omi Konan Sanyare Odori - In addition to the Kozue Festival, it includes festivals in Kusatsu City. Designated on March 16, 2020 (Reiwa 2) . Owned by the Kozue Festival Preservation Society. {{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}} It has been designated as one of the {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}}. === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Festival performing arts of the Kozue Festival - Owned by the Kozue Festival Preservation Society. === Others === * Mikoshi decoration phoenix and wig *: Made in the Muromachi period. The phoenix and {{jalink|huawig|Q6386848}} displayed on the {{jalink|portable shrine|Q979940}} are both made of cast bronze. The former is 54.0 cm in height, and the latter is 16.7 cm in length and 15.5 cm in width {{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=315}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ritto City|Q857310}}Shimotoyama 1200 === Access === * From {{jalink|JR West||JR}}{{jalink|Kusatsu Line|Q865516}}{{jalink|Tehara Station|Q5362482}}, take the {{jalink|Kuri-chan Bus|Q11265638}} (Jitakinakatsu Line) and get off at Shimotoyama Miyagaya stop, then walk 3 minutes. * 10 minutes by car from {{jalink|Meishin Expressway|Q904481}}{{jalink|Ritto Interchange|Q11110683|Ritto IC}}. === Surroundings === * {{jalink|Shimotoyama Tumulus|Q60990033}} - Chieftain's tomb from the end of the 4th century to the beginning of the 5th century. A {{jalink|circular tomb|Q11394747}} with a diameter of 55 meters. Ritto City designated historic site. * Jiyama Tumulus - A chieftain's tomb dating from the first half of the 5th century, second only to Shimotoyama Tumulus. {{jalink|scallop-shaped tumulus|Q11480883}} with a length of 90 meters. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Original source === {{reflist|group="hara"}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} === Old {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Other names = Ozuesha, Kozue Shrine, Kozue Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 5th|Q2550}} (Kozue Festival) |Shinto ritual = Flower umbrella dance (May 5th) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Kotsuki Taisha|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Otsuki-taisha torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]]'''Kotsuki Taisha''' The main shrine"/>) is located in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ritto City|Q857310}}. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. {{jalink|Divine Crest}} is "Kakudari wisteria" and "Maya no Usagi" [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Otsuki Taisha] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency). . The ``{{jalink|Sanyare Odori of Omi Konan}}'' held within the temple grounds is designated as one of {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}} which is {{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Rakubetsuou||Ochibetsu no Mikoto}} (Ochiwake no Mikoto) Explanation board inside the precincts. *: Also written as ``Rakubetsu no Mikoto'' or ``Sobetsu no Mikoto''. He was the {{jalink|prince|prince|Q12886381}} of the 11th {{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}} and the ancestor of {{jalink|Kotsukiyama-kun|Q11462129}} ({{jalink|Kotsuki clan|Q11462133}}). Depending on the literature, the notation ``{{jalink|Issokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324}}'' is also seen, but Issokubetsu no Mikoto is a half-brother of Ochibetsu no Mikoto. * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Oonamuchi no Mikoto}} *: In the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, there was a {{jalink|Tendai sect|Q668188}} Gakuon-ji Temple (Kakuon-ji Temple) adjacent to the precincts of Otsuki Taisha Shrine; It is presumed that it was {{jalink|solicited}} and {{jalink|enshrined|Q11412524}} from {{jalink|Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|Hiyoshi Nishimoto Shrine}}. ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, there is one deity enshrined. At Kotsuki Taisha Shrine, two wooden seated statues of male deities (Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue and Den Omi Taka no Mikoto statue) are said to have been made in the early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}}, and by this time the enshrined deities were the current two. === History === The detailed founding date is unknown, but according to the shrine's legend, it was built in {{jalink|Ancient|Q41493}} in {{jalink|Kurita County|Q6446084}} (currently {{jalink|Kusatsu City|Q386677}}). Our company's origins date back to the time when {{jalink|Otsukiyama-kun|Q11462129}} (Otsukiyama-kun), a powerful family from the Ritto City|Q857310}} area, enshrined his ancestral deity, {{jalink|Rakubetsu-o||Ochibetsu no Mikoto}}.[http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency Kotsuki Taisha].Otsukiyama-kun was an ancient powerful family in Kurita County, and it is said that he also presented {{jalink|Uname|Q11644506}} to the {{jalink|Imperial Court (Japan)|Q30253177|Imperial Court}}. In addition to the Kotsuki Taisha tumulus group remaining within the Otsuki Taisha campus, the surrounding area is the {{jalink|Shimotoyama Tumulus|Q60990033}} (a tangible {{jalink|cultural property designated by Ritto City)}. |Q858308}}), the Jiyama Tumulus, and the Oka Ruins (Kurita{{jalink|gunga|Q11643230}} ruins) remain, and these are considered to be historical sites related to Kotsukiyama-kun. Based on these, it is assumed that Otsukiyama-kun was a member of the Kurita County {{jalink|gunji|Q4152149}} class. Otsukiyama-kun was {{jalink|Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}15th year ({{jalink|873|Q30369}})"Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated December 2, 15th year of Jogan (873). In , he moved to {{jalink|Heiankyo|Q751907}} and later served the {{jalink|Imperial Court (Japan)|Q30253177|Imperial Court}} as the {{jalink|Kotsuki clan|Q11462133}} ({{jalink|official|Q11452088}} family).In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushu}}, the divine names such as ``Kojogami'', ``Kojogami'', and ``Shōjogami'' were used in the 5th year of Jogan ({{jalink|863|Q25052}}) {{jalink|Shinkai|Q11591025}} was ranked Jugoinoge (Junior Fifth Rank) in 7th year of Jogan ({{jalink|865|Q30350}}) and to the lower rank of Junior Fourth Rank (Junior Fourth Rank) in {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}}11 ({{jalink|911|Q24062}}){{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}.{{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) In the Kishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|Shinmeicho}}, "Kotsuki Taisha" is written in {{jalink|Omi Province|Q843882}}{{jalink|Kurita County|Q6446084}}, It is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} along with its associated shrine {{jalink|Otsuki Shrine|Q11462132}} (currently {{jalink|Kusatsu City|Q386677}} Aochi Town). {Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}The ``taisha'' in the company name ``Kotsuki Taisha'' appears to be to distinguish it from the Otsuki Shrine. {{Harv|About the mathematician Dr. Kotsuki Imao|2002}}. . The company name is pronounced as ``Wotsuki no Ohoyashiro'' {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Additionally, two wooden male god seated statues ({{jalink|Important Cultural Properties|Q1188622}}) made in the early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}} of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} have survived to this day.After the Otsuki clan moved to the center, the head of the shrine was revered and renovated by the {{jalink|Aochi clan|Q11661125}}, who had a base in the vicinity, and the current inner palace of the main shrine was built by Motoji Aochi in October 1281|Q5537}}.. {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} In April 2013 ({{jalink|1343|Q6072}}), a four-legged gate was built by Shigeyori Aochi (although it does not exist, only the ridgepole was used as the main hall) {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shinouchi Shrine)|1981}}. According to "{{jalink|Sono Tairyaku|Q11422787}}" , in December of the same year, he was granted the ultimate rank of Shoichii upon the application of Shigeyori Aochi (Shigeyori Minamoto) {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. After that, in {{jalink|Eisho|Q1193577}}16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}), the current main shrine (Important Cultural Property) was rebuilt by Makoto Aojima. However, the Aochi clan fell into decline during the {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}} with the decline of the master family {{jalink|Rokkaku clan|Q2975342}}.In {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}13 ({{jalink|1608|Q6758}}) 3}} There was a donation of rice fields from the lord {{jalink|Ujitetsu Toda|Q3530228}}, and thereafter it was relieved by the {{jalink|black seal|Q11678034}}{{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}9) {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534} }, it was ranked as {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} and was promoted to {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in {{jalink|1881|Q7808}} (Meiji 14). === God level === * Record of {{jalink|Divine Rank|Q11591025}} dedication in the {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}} era ** {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 5th year ({{jalink|863 years|Q25052}}) December 3rd, Jugoinoge ("{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated December 3, 863. - The notation is "Shojo God". ** December 5th, 7th year of the Jogan era ({{jalink|865|Q30350}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper middle fifth rank (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') - The spelling is "Kojogami". ** October 9, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}} 6th year ({{jalink|882|Q23824}}), from the upper middle fifth rank to the upper middle fifth rank {{Sub|Mama}} (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') - The spelling is ``Koshogami''. Is it a mistake of Shogoinoge? {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * After Six Kingdoms History** {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} February 2nd, 11th year ({{jalink|911|Q24062}}), Junior Fourth Rank Lower ({{jalink|Nihon Kiryaku|Q1053542}}) - The spelling is ``Koshogami''. ** {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} December 2nd year ({{jalink|1343|Q6072}}), Shoichii ("{{jalink|Sono Tairyaku|Q11422787}}") - The notation is "Kozuesha". === Precincts === * Main shrine ({{jalink|Important cultural property|Q1188622}}) - Rebuilt in the late {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. The roof is {{jalink|Japanese cypress bark |Q90373477}}. The overall structure is simple but strong, with thick {{jalink|wood splitting}} and few carved decorations, as well as old-style boat elbows for the kumihimo. The copy of the ridge plate and the ink calligraphy on the ridgepole mention that it was rebuilt by Makoto Aojima in {{jalink|Eisho|Q1193577}}16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}), and the style suggests that it dates back to that time. Along with the main shrine, two ridge plates have also been passed down, each marking the replacement of the roof in {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}8 ({{jalink|1723|Q6921}}) and {{jalink|Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}12 ({{jalink|1815|Q6948}}). A palace is placed in the inner sanctum of the main hall, and two wooden seated statues of male deities (Important Cultural Properties) are enshrined within the palace. The palace has an ink calligraphy dated to {{jalink|Koan|Q389180}}4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), which is older than the main shrine. This main shrine, along with the palace and two ridge plates (both designated), have been designated as important cultural properties.* The Hall - Built in {{jalink|1956|Q5221}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}31). The roof is made of cypress bark. * Worship hall - Reconstruction of {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}. {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri |Q855025}}, the roof is tiled {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. * Ichino Torii - Built in {{jalink|1930|Q18787}} (1930). Regarding the torii, there is an old record that it was built in {{jalink|Enkyo|Q1183728}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1745|Q7586}}) near the current {{jalink|Meishin Expressway|Q904481}}, and its foundation stone was relocated to Otabisho and is from the stone marker next to the existing Ichi-no-Torii. . * Otabisho (Wakamiyasha) - Located in Oka, Ritto City, adjacent to the Oka ruins ({{jalink|Kurita-gun#gunga|Q6446084|Kurita-gunga ruins}}) ({{Coord|35|00|37.81 |N|135|59|07.23|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Otabisho}}). * Kotsuki Taisha Tumulus Group - Within the precincts are {{jalink|circular tombs| Q11394747}}・{{jalink|Hangular tomb|Q11504353}}There are over 10 {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha kofun group (Zoku kofun)|2002}}.** Mound No. 1 - The largest round tomb among the tombs, with a diameter of 25 meters. The stone remaining on the mound indicates the possibility of {{jalink|horizontal stone chamber|Q11543435}}{{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha kofun group (Zoku kofun)|2002}}. ** Mound No. 10 - Located on the ridge west of our company grounds. It is a rectangular tomb measuring 15.5 meters from north to south and 13.5 meters from east to west, and is said to have been constructed in the latter half of the 5th century. {{jalink|1990|Q2064}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}2) and {{jalink|1999|Q2091}} (Heisei 11) Excavations were carried out twice in 2002), and two split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins, an iron sword and a comb, and a Sue ware from the surrounding moat were unearthed. The details of the other burial mounds are not clear {{Sfn|Otsuki Taisha Tumulus Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}. The people buried in these burial mounds are believed to be members of the Otsukiyama clan. === Setsusha === * Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine) - Enshrined on the right side facing the main shrine. * Hiyoshi Shrine (Sanno Shrine) - Enshrined on the left facing the main shrine. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined next to the approach. * Ryuosha - Enshrined on top of the Kotsuki Taisha burial mound. * Outside company ** Junisho Shrine - Enshrined in Mt. Shimoto, Ritto City ({{Coord|35|00|17.43|N|135|59|28.81|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Outside Shrine: Jūnisho Shrine}}). The precincts are said to be the site of Gakuonji Temple (Kakuonji Temple). *** Shrines within the precincts of Junisho Shrine: Bentensha, Gionsha ** Hachiman Shrine File:Otsuki-taisha haiden.JPG|Shrine of worship File:Otsuki-taisha Ryuou-sha.JPG|Legendary ancient tomb stone (front) and Ryuou-sha shrine in the precincts File:Otsuki-taisha ichinotorii.JPG|Ichino Torii File:Otsuki-taisha otabisho.JPG|Otabisho (Wakamiyasha) File:Otsuki-taisha Yasaka-sha.JPG|Yasakasha (Gionsha) File:Otsuki-taisha Hiyoshi-sha.JPG|Hiyoshisha (Sannosha) File:Otsuki-taisha Inari-sha.JPG|Inari-sha File:Junisho Shrine (Kotsuki Taisha shrine).JPG|Junisho Shrine (outside shrine) === Main festivals === The annual festival held on {{jalink|May 5th|Q2550}} is commonly known as the ``''Kozue Festival''''. In ancient times, the first day of the rabbit was {{jalink|Uzuki|Q9092155}}, but since {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}42), the funeral ceremony has been held on May 5th.[http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Otsuki Taisha] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency). . The {{jalink|Hanagasa Odori|Q11615628|Hanagasa Odori}}, which is the main event of the festival, is currently held in rotation in five regions, but {{jalink|Eitoku|Q1145 According to a board dated 805}}2 ({{jalink|1382|Q6335}}), the history of Ritto is said to have been carried out by an influential family called the ``Sakakimoto family'' in ancient times. Volume 4|1994|p=281}}.During the festival, a mikoshi (portable shrine) is carried in procession to {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}} near the Oka Ruins (Kurita Gunga Ruins), and Hanagasa Odori (Flower Umbrella Dance) is dedicated at several locations, including in front of the main shrine of Otsuki Taisha Shrine and Otabisho. The centerpiece of this Hanagasa Odori is a child actor wearing a charming costume, followed by an adult holding a kasahoko (flower umbrella), who dance and sing in a spectacular manner. This is said to be a modernized version of the Taiko drum dance. The Kozue Festival was selected as an intangible folk cultural property (selected intangible folk cultural property) in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}} in {{jalink|1988|Q2426}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}63) and was designated as an intangible folk cultural property (selected intangible folk cultural property) for which measures such as recording should be taken in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. Volume 4|1994|p=281}}, {{jalink|2020|Q25337}} ({{jalink|Reiwa|Q62609017}}2) includes festivals in Kusatsu City and ``{{jalink|Sanyare in Omi Konan. The dance}} has been designated as a national important intangible folk cultural property. Cultural Affairs Council Report (Designation of Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties, etc.)] (Agency for Cultural Affairs press release, January 17, 2020) . Furthermore, "Omi Konan Sanyare Odori" will be held on November 30th, {{jalink|2022|Q49625}} (Reiwa 4).{{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7 809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}} It was designated as one of the {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}}. === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Main shrine (1 attached palace, 2 ridge tags) (building) *: Built in the late {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. {{jalink|1958|Q5253}} Designated on May 14, 1958 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|102|1438|Kotsuki Taisha main shrine}}. * 2 wooden seated male god statues (sculpture) *: {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}}. Two statues, the Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue and the Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue. {{jalink|1997|Q2088}} Designated on June 30, 1997 {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|5159|Wooden Seated Statue of the Male God (Denrakubetsu no Mikoto)}}{{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|5160|Wooden Seated Statue of the Male God (Dennen Omi Takamikoto)}}. ** The main deity statue, Denrakubetsu no Mikoto, is made of {{jalink|Hinoki|Q917187}} wood and is 60.6 cm in height. His belongings, the scepter and scepter, were made of a different material, but they were lost. He wears a crown on his head and a vermillion robe.{{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=281}}. ** The statue of Omi Takamiko is made of a single piece of cypress and is 48.8 centimeters tall. His belongings, the scepter and scepter, were made of a different material, but they were lost. He wears a wide crown, a beard, and a green robe.{{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=281}}. === Important intangible folk cultural property === * Omi Konan Sanyare Odori - In addition to the Kozue Festival, it includes festivals in Kusatsu City. Designated on March 16, 2020 (Reiwa 2) . Owned by the Kozue Festival Preservation Society. {{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}} It has been designated as one of the {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}}. === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Festival performing arts of the Kozue Festival - Owned by the Kozue Festival Preservation Society. === Others === * Mikoshi decoration phoenix and wig *: Made in the Muromachi period. The phoenix and {{jalink|huawig|Q6386848}} displayed on the {{jalink|portable shrine|Q979940}} are both made of cast bronze. The former is 54.0 cm in height, and the latter is 16.7 cm in length and 15.5 cm in width {{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=315}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ritto City|Q857310}}Shimotoyama 1200 === Access === * From {{jalink|JR West||JR}}{{jalink|Kusatsu Line|Q865516}}{{jalink|Tehara Station|Q5362482}}, take the {{jalink|Kuri-chan Bus|Q11265638}} (Jitakinakatsu Line) and get off at Shimotoyama Miyagaya stop, then walk 3 minutes. * 10 minutes by car from {{jalink|Meishin Expressway|Q904481}}{{jalink|Ritto Interchange|Q11110683|Ritto IC}}. === Surroundings === * {{jalink|Shimotoyama Tumulus|Q60990033}} - Chieftain's tomb from the end of the 4th century to the beginning of the 5th century. A {{jalink|circular tomb|Q11394747}} with a diameter of 55 meters. Ritto City designated historic site. * Jiyama Tumulus - A chieftain's tomb dating from the first half of the 5th century, second only to Shimotoyama Tumulus. {{jalink|scallop-shaped tumulus|Q11480883}} with a length of 90 meters. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Original source === {{reflist|group="hara"}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} === Old {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Other names = Ozuesha, Kozue Shrine, Kozue Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 5th|Q2550}} (Kozue Festival) |Shinto ritual = Flower umbrella dance (May 5th) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Kotsuki Taisha|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Otsuki-taisha torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]]'''Kotsuki Taisha''' The main shrine"/>) is located in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ritto City|Q857310}}. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. {{jalink|Divine Crest}} is "Kakudari wisteria" and "Maya no Usagi" [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Otsuki Taisha] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency). . The ``{{jalink|Sanyare Odori of Omi Konan}}'' held within the temple grounds is designated as one of {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}} which is {{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Rakubetsuou||Ochibetsu no Mikoto}} (Ochiwake no Mikoto) Explanation board inside the precincts. *: Also written as ``Rakubetsu no Mikoto'' or ``Sobetsu no Mikoto''. He was the {{jalink|prince|prince|Q12886381}} of the 11th {{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}} and the ancestor of {{jalink|Kotsukiyama-kun|Q11462129}} ({{jalink|Kotsuki clan|Q11462133}}). Depending on the literature, the notation ``{{jalink|Issokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324}}'' is also seen, but Issokubetsu no Mikoto is a half-brother of Ochibetsu no Mikoto. * {{jalink|Okuninushi|Q276944|Oonamuchi no Mikoto}} *: In the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, there was a {{jalink|Tendai sect|Q668188}} Gakuon-ji Temple (Kakuon-ji Temple) adjacent to the precincts of Otsuki Taisha Shrine; It is presumed that it was {{jalink|solicited}} and {{jalink|enshrined|Q11412524}} from {{jalink|Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|Hiyoshi Nishimoto Shrine}}. ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}, there is one deity enshrined. At Kotsuki Taisha Shrine, two wooden seated statues of male deities (Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue and Den Omi Taka no Mikoto statue) are said to have been made in the early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}}, and by this time the enshrined deities were the current two. === History === The detailed founding date is unknown, but according to the shrine's legend, it was built in {{jalink|Ancient|Q41493}} in {{jalink|Kurita County|Q6446084}} (currently {{jalink|Kusatsu City|Q386677}}). Our company's origins date back to the time when {{jalink|Otsukiyama-kun|Q11462129}} (Otsukiyama-kun), a powerful family from the Ritto City|Q857310}} area, enshrined his ancestral deity, {{jalink|Rakubetsu-o||Ochibetsu no Mikoto}}.[http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency Kotsuki Taisha].Otsukiyama-kun was an ancient powerful family in Kurita County, and it is said that he also presented {{jalink|Uname|Q11644506}} to the {{jalink|Imperial Court (Japan)|Q30253177|Imperial Court}}. In addition to the Kotsuki Taisha tumulus group remaining within the Otsuki Taisha campus, the surrounding area is the {{jalink|Shimotoyama Tumulus|Q60990033}} (a tangible {{jalink|cultural property designated by Ritto City)}. |Q858308}}), the Jiyama Tumulus, and the Oka Ruins (Kurita{{jalink|gunga|Q11643230}} ruins) remain, and these are considered to be historical sites related to Kotsukiyama-kun. Based on these, it is assumed that Otsukiyama-kun was a member of the Kurita County {{jalink|gunji|Q4152149}} class. Otsukiyama-kun was {{jalink|Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}15th year ({{jalink|873|Q30369}})"Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated December 2, 15th year of Jogan (873). In , he moved to {{jalink|Heiankyo|Q751907}} and later served the {{jalink|Imperial Court (Japan)|Q30253177|Imperial Court}} as the {{jalink|Kotsuki clan|Q11462133}} ({{jalink|official|Q11452088}} family).In {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushu}}, the divine names such as ``Kojogami'', ``Kojogami'', and ``Shōjogami'' were used in the 5th year of Jogan ({{jalink|863|Q25052}}) {{jalink|Shinkai|Q11591025}} was ranked Jugoinoge (Junior Fifth Rank) in 7th year of Jogan ({{jalink|865|Q30350}}) and to the lower rank of Junior Fourth Rank (Junior Fourth Rank) in {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}}11 ({{jalink|911|Q24062}}){{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Heibonsha)|1991}}.{{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) In the Kishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|Shinmeicho}}, "Kotsuki Taisha" is written in {{jalink|Omi Province|Q843882}}{{jalink|Kurita County|Q6446084}}, It is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} along with its associated shrine {{jalink|Otsuki Shrine|Q11462132}} (currently {{jalink|Kusatsu City|Q386677}} Aochi Town). {Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}The ``taisha'' in the company name ``Kotsuki Taisha'' appears to be to distinguish it from the Otsuki Shrine. {{Harv|About the mathematician Dr. Kotsuki Imao|2002}}. . The company name is pronounced as ``Wotsuki no Ohoyashiro'' {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}. Additionally, two wooden male god seated statues ({{jalink|Important Cultural Properties|Q1188622}}) made in the early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}} of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} have survived to this day.After the Otsuki clan moved to the center, the head of the shrine was revered and renovated by the {{jalink|Aochi clan|Q11661125}}, who had a base in the vicinity, and the current inner palace of the main shrine was built by Motoji Aochi in October 1281|Q5537}}.. {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} In April 2013 ({{jalink|1343|Q6072}}), a four-legged gate was built by Shigeyori Aochi (although it does not exist, only the ridgepole was used as the main hall) {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shinouchi Shrine)|1981}}. According to "{{jalink|Sono Tairyaku|Q11422787}}" , in December of the same year, he was granted the ultimate rank of Shoichii upon the application of Shigeyori Aochi (Shigeyori Minamoto) {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. After that, in {{jalink|Eisho|Q1193577}}16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}), the current main shrine (Important Cultural Property) was rebuilt by Makoto Aojima. However, the Aochi clan fell into decline during the {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}} with the decline of the master family {{jalink|Rokkaku clan|Q2975342}}.In {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}13 ({{jalink|1608|Q6758}}) 3}} There was a donation of rice fields from the lord {{jalink|Ujitetsu Toda|Q3530228}}, and thereafter it was relieved by the {{jalink|black seal|Q11678034}}{{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}9) {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534} }, it was ranked as {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} and was promoted to {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in {{jalink|1881|Q7808}} (Meiji 14). === God level === * Record of {{jalink|Divine Rank|Q11591025}} dedication in the {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}} era ** {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 5th year ({{jalink|863 years|Q25052}}) December 3rd, Jugoinoge ("{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}") "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated December 3, 863. - The notation is "Shojo God". ** December 5th, 7th year of the Jogan era ({{jalink|865|Q30350}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper middle fifth rank (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') - The spelling is "Kojogami". ** October 9, {{jalink|Genkei|Q747451}} 6th year ({{jalink|882|Q23824}}), from the upper middle fifth rank to the upper middle fifth rank {{Sub|Mama}} (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') - The spelling is ``Koshogami''. Is it a mistake of Shogoinoge? {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha (Heibonsha)|1991}}. * After Six Kingdoms History** {{jalink|Engi|Q1374325}} February 2nd, 11th year ({{jalink|911|Q24062}}), Junior Fourth Rank Lower ({{jalink|Nihon Kiryaku|Q1053542}}) - The spelling is ``Koshogami''. ** {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} December 2nd year ({{jalink|1343|Q6072}}), Shoichii ("{{jalink|Sono Tairyaku|Q11422787}}") - The notation is "Kozuesha". === Precincts === * Main shrine ({{jalink|Important cultural property|Q1188622}}) - Rebuilt in the late {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. The roof is {{jalink|Japanese cypress bark |Q90373477}}. The overall structure is simple but strong, with thick {{jalink|wood splitting}} and few carved decorations, as well as old-style boat elbows for the kumihimo. The copy of the ridge plate and the ink calligraphy on the ridgepole mention that it was rebuilt by Makoto Aojima in {{jalink|Eisho|Q1193577}}16 ({{jalink|1519|Q6278}}), and the style suggests that it dates back to that time. Along with the main shrine, two ridge plates have also been passed down, each marking the replacement of the roof in {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}8 ({{jalink|1723|Q6921}}) and {{jalink|Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}12 ({{jalink|1815|Q6948}}). A palace is placed in the inner sanctum of the main hall, and two wooden seated statues of male deities (Important Cultural Properties) are enshrined within the palace. The palace has an ink calligraphy dated to {{jalink|Koan|Q389180}}4 ({{jalink|1281|Q5537}}), which is older than the main shrine. This main shrine, along with the palace and two ridge plates (both designated), have been designated as important cultural properties.* The Hall - Built in {{jalink|1956|Q5221}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}31). The roof is made of cypress bark. * Worship hall - Reconstruction of {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}. {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri |Q855025}}, the roof is tiled {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1981}}. * Ichino Torii - Built in {{jalink|1930|Q18787}} (1930). Regarding the torii, there is an old record that it was built in {{jalink|Enkyo|Q1183728}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1745|Q7586}}) near the current {{jalink|Meishin Expressway|Q904481}}, and its foundation stone was relocated to Otabisho and is from the stone marker next to the existing Ichi-no-Torii. . * Otabisho (Wakamiyasha) - Located in Oka, Ritto City, adjacent to the Oka ruins ({{jalink|Kurita-gun#gunga|Q6446084|Kurita-gunga ruins}}) ({{Coord|35|00|37.81 |N|135|59|07.23|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Otabisho}}). * Kotsuki Taisha Tumulus Group - Within the precincts are {{jalink|circular tombs| Q11394747}}・{{jalink|Hangular tomb|Q11504353}}There are over 10 {{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha kofun group (Zoku kofun)|2002}}.** Mound No. 1 - The largest round tomb among the tombs, with a diameter of 25 meters. The stone remaining on the mound indicates the possibility of {{jalink|horizontal stone chamber|Q11543435}}{{Sfn|Kotsuki Taisha kofun group (Zoku kofun)|2002}}. ** Mound No. 10 - Located on the ridge west of our company grounds. It is a rectangular tomb measuring 15.5 meters from north to south and 13.5 meters from east to west, and is said to have been constructed in the latter half of the 5th century. {{jalink|1990|Q2064}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}2) and {{jalink|1999|Q2091}} (Heisei 11) Excavations were carried out twice in 2002), and two split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins, an iron sword and a comb, and a Sue ware from the surrounding moat were unearthed. The details of the other burial mounds are not clear {{Sfn|Otsuki Taisha Tumulus Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}. The people buried in these burial mounds are believed to be members of the Otsukiyama clan. === Setsusha === * Yasaka Shrine (Gion Shrine) - Enshrined on the right side facing the main shrine. * Hiyoshi Shrine (Sanno Shrine) - Enshrined on the left facing the main shrine. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined next to the approach. * Ryuosha - Enshrined on top of the Kotsuki Taisha burial mound. * Outside company ** Junisho Shrine - Enshrined in Mt. Shimoto, Ritto City ({{Coord|35|00|17.43|N|135|59|28.81|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Outside Shrine: Jūnisho Shrine}}). The precincts are said to be the site of Gakuonji Temple (Kakuonji Temple). *** Shrines within the precincts of Junisho Shrine: Bentensha, Gionsha ** Hachiman Shrine File:Otsuki-taisha haiden.JPG|Shrine of worship File:Otsuki-taisha Ryuou-sha.JPG|Legendary ancient tomb stone (front) and Ryuou-sha shrine in the precincts File:Otsuki-taisha ichinotorii.JPG|Ichino Torii File:Otsuki-taisha otabisho.JPG|Otabisho (Wakamiyasha) File:Otsuki-taisha Yasaka-sha.JPG|Yasakasha (Gionsha) File:Otsuki-taisha Hiyoshi-sha.JPG|Hiyoshisha (Sannosha) File:Otsuki-taisha Inari-sha.JPG|Inari-sha File:Junisho Shrine (Kotsuki Taisha shrine).JPG|Junisho Shrine (outside shrine) === Main festivals === The annual festival held on {{jalink|May 5th|Q2550}} is commonly known as the ``''Kozue Festival''''. In ancient times, the first day of the rabbit was {{jalink|Uzuki|Q9092155}}, but since {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}42), the funeral ceremony has been held on May 5th.[http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=368 Otsuki Taisha] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency). . The {{jalink|Hanagasa Odori|Q11615628|Hanagasa Odori}}, which is the main event of the festival, is currently held in rotation in five regions, but {{jalink|Eitoku|Q1145 According to a board dated 805}}2 ({{jalink|1382|Q6335}}), the history of Ritto is said to have been carried out by an influential family called the ``Sakakimoto family'' in ancient times. Volume 4|1994|p=281}}.During the festival, a mikoshi (portable shrine) is carried in procession to {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}} near the Oka Ruins (Kurita Gunga Ruins), and Hanagasa Odori (Flower Umbrella Dance) is dedicated at several locations, including in front of the main shrine of Otsuki Taisha Shrine and Otabisho. The centerpiece of this Hanagasa Odori is a child actor wearing a charming costume, followed by an adult holding a kasahoko (flower umbrella), who dance and sing in a spectacular manner. This is said to be a modernized version of the Taiko drum dance. The Kozue Festival was selected as an intangible folk cultural property (selected intangible folk cultural property) in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}} in {{jalink|1988|Q2426}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}63) and was designated as an intangible folk cultural property (selected intangible folk cultural property) for which measures such as recording should be taken in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}. Volume 4|1994|p=281}}, {{jalink|2020|Q25337}} ({{jalink|Reiwa|Q62609017}}2) includes festivals in Kusatsu City and ``{{jalink|Sanyare in Omi Konan. The dance}} has been designated as a national important intangible folk cultural property. Cultural Affairs Council Report (Designation of Important Tangible Folk Cultural Properties, etc.)] (Agency for Cultural Affairs press release, January 17, 2020) . Furthermore, "Omi Konan Sanyare Odori" will be held on November 30th, {{jalink|2022|Q49625}} (Reiwa 4).{{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7 809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}} It was designated as one of the {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}}. === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Main shrine (1 attached palace, 2 ridge tags) (building) *: Built in the late {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. {{jalink|1958|Q5253}} Designated on May 14, 1958 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|102|1438|Kotsuki Taisha main shrine}}. * 2 wooden seated male god statues (sculpture) *: {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, early {{jalink|11th century|Q7063}}. Two statues, the Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue and the Denrakubetsu no Mikoto statue. {{jalink|1997|Q2088}} Designated on June 30, 1997 {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|5159|Wooden Seated Statue of the Male God (Denrakubetsu no Mikoto)}}{{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|5160|Wooden Seated Statue of the Male God (Dennen Omi Takamikoto)}}. ** The main deity statue, Denrakubetsu no Mikoto, is made of {{jalink|Hinoki|Q917187}} wood and is 60.6 cm in height. His belongings, the scepter and scepter, were made of a different material, but they were lost. He wears a crown on his head and a vermillion robe.{{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=281}}. ** The statue of Omi Takamiko is made of a single piece of cypress and is 48.8 centimeters tall. His belongings, the scepter and scepter, were made of a different material, but they were lost. He wears a wide crown, a beard, and a green robe.{{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=281}}. === Important intangible folk cultural property === * Omi Konan Sanyare Odori - In addition to the Kozue Festival, it includes festivals in Kusatsu City. Designated on March 16, 2020 (Reiwa 2) . Owned by the Kozue Festival Preservation Society. {{jalink|United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|Q7809|UNESCO}}{{jalink|Intangible Cultural Heritage|Q84036549}} It has been designated as one of the {{jalink|Furyu-odori|Q11665528}}. === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Festival performing arts of the Kozue Festival - Owned by the Kozue Festival Preservation Society. === Others === * Mikoshi decoration phoenix and wig *: Made in the Muromachi period. The phoenix and {{jalink|huawig|Q6386848}} displayed on the {{jalink|portable shrine|Q979940}} are both made of cast bronze. The former is 54.0 cm in height, and the latter is 16.7 cm in length and 15.5 cm in width {{Sfn|History of Ritto Volume 4|1994|p=315}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Ritto City|Q857310}}Shimotoyama 1200 === Access === * From {{jalink|JR West||JR}}{{jalink|Kusatsu Line|Q865516}}{{jalink|Tehara Station|Q5362482}}, take the {{jalink|Kuri-chan Bus|Q11265638}} (Jitakinakatsu Line) and get off at Shimotoyama Miyagaya stop, then walk 3 minutes. * 10 minutes by car from {{jalink|Meishin Expressway|Q904481}}{{jalink|Ritto Interchange|Q11110683|Ritto IC}}. === Surroundings === * {{jalink|Shimotoyama Tumulus|Q60990033}} - Chieftain's tomb from the end of the 4th century to the beginning of the 5th century. A {{jalink|circular tomb|Q11394747}} with a diameter of 55 meters. Ritto City designated historic site. * Jiyama Tumulus - A chieftain's tomb dating from the first half of the 5th century, second only to Shimotoyama Tumulus. {{jalink|scallop-shaped tumulus|Q11480883}} with a length of 90 meters. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Original source === {{reflist|group="hara"}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} ===
